issue_title: Curio-Blender Bridge
issue_body:
Probably this deserve its own project repository, but this issue would serve
as a infoboard about one of Curio's integration possibilities.

Quick summary:

- Blender: www.blender.org

- Currently threaded python scripts can not safely access Blender internals,
since it is no thread safety. See: Strange errors using ‘threading’ module
https://docs.blender.org/api/blender_python_api_current/info_gotcha.html

- Non threaded scripts cause Blender to wait for script to finish which
results in all other functionality (especially GUI) to freeze.

- One temporary solution is here. It uses a mix of execution time sharing
using some capabilities within blender.
https://github.com/akloster/blender-asyncio
https://blenderartists.org/forum/showthread.php?405124-Running-background-jobs-with-Asyncio

- An other solution is for headless blender: Python script owns
the main thread and can perform however it likes. Naturally
there can not be any gui. All operations can be done via
rest gateway/backdoor.
https://github.com/z25/BgeHttpServer

- With the rise of networked game engines, collabrative modelling,
and perhaps networked CAD engines, there is certainly interesting
opportunity. There is some blender community inertia about
such fields. Perhaps we may expect proper (and safe) engine access
support on blender for coming releases. But even without it above
approached may certainly employ Curio.

issue_title: [discussion] Should functions like spawn take a coroutine object, or the classic fn+args+kwargs tuple?
issue_body:
Right now, `curio.run` and `curio.spawn` take a coroutine object, i.e., the return value from calling an async function:
```python
async def my_async_function(...):
    ...
curio.spawn(my_async_function(arg1, arg2))
```

This is fairly elegant and I'm not sure it's wrong, but I've run into a few situations where it's feeling a bit awkward, so I wanted to raise for the discussion the possibility that they should instead take an async callable + arguments:
```python
curio.spawn(my_async_function, arg1, arg2)
```

The arguments for taking a callable:

* It's more parallel to the non-async/await way of doing things, which is nice for teaching and general simplicity. (I think this is more important than it might look: if it weren't for `curio.run` and `curio.spawn`, you could get a *long* way with beginners pretending that `await f(...)` is a single indivisible piece of syntax, an "async call". Obviously you want to break this down eventually, but `curio.run` and `curio.spawn` force you to do it immediately.)
* In some cases, the function that takes the coroutine might actually *decide* whether or not to actually run it based on some logic. But the CPython runtime is quite certain that if you ever create a coroutine object, you are definitely going to run it, and it will yell at you if you don't. (A case that I ran into is a supervisor class's `spawn_supervised(coro)` method, which might error out if the supervisor is not running. Of course my tests have to invoke this, so get an unavoidable warning...) For consistency, it would be nice if these functions used the same calling convention as `curio.spawn`.
* In some cases, the function that takes the coroutine might need to be able to run it multiple times. Examples would include `curio.tcp_server`, or the `spawn_supervised` method on a task supervisor that supports automatically restarted crashed tasks (like Erlang supervisors do). For consistency, it would be nice if these functions used the same calling convention as `curio.spawn`.

The arguments against taking a callable:

* Breaks compatibility, but eh, that's why this is "experimental".
* It's nice to collect up the fn/args/kwargs into a single object, so as to avoid confusion between arguments-for-the-function versus arguments-for-`run`-or-`spawn`. In particular, if we actually made this change, then probably the best way to do it would be to make the signature of `run` and `spawn` be:
  ```python
  def run(fn, *args, *, ... kwargs for run ...):
      ...
  ```
  i.e., positional arguments belong to the function, but kwargs are reserved for `run` or `spawn`. Or we might not even want to allow positional args at all, and just make it a bare zero-args callable:
  ```python
  def run(fn, *, ... kwargs for run ...):
     ...
  ```

  The counter-argument to this is that we already have perfectly nice and idiomatic ways in Python to bundle up fn/args/kwargs together:
  ```python
  curio.run(partial(my_async_function, arg1, arg2))
  ```
  And we have to use this style anyway for things like `tcp_server`, so we might as well use it everywhere.

issue_body: I have installed dlib with AVX_INSTRUCTIONS and CUDA+cuDNN. issue_title: The documentation should be clear of whether password can be bytes or only unicode
issue_body:
All the below applies to Python 3 only.
Some backends (i.e. SecretStorage) accept bytestrings as passwords, others (the file backend) do not.

But there is some code that tries to use bytestrings as passwords and [fails](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python-keyring/+bug/1685547) with the file backend.

We should either fix the file backend (which is now in `keyrings.alt`) and make sure all other backends support bytes too, or fix the documentation to clearly say that the password should be a unicode string.

issue_title: throttle behavior different from lodash: no trailing edge call
issue_body:
When using throttle from lodash, if one or more calls are made during the wait period, a single call will be scheduled at the end of the wait period (i.e. the trailing edge). For example:

```var throttle = required('lodash.throttle')
const throttled = throttle(() => console.log("called"), 5000)
for (var i=0; i < 3; i++) throttled()
```

You should see the throttled function called twice. However, with pydash, I'm only seeing a single call. If using to check for updates to a resource, you might miss the last update as a result. Here's the equivalent pydash example that I tried:

```from pydash import throttle
throttled = throttle(lambda: print("called"), 5000)
[throttled() for _ in range(3)]```

Just taking a quick look at the source, pydash's debounce is lacking lodash's leading / trailing options. If those were present and implemented, throttle could just be rewritten as a call to debounce (which is how lodash's throttle is done).

issue_title: intel hex Extended Linear Address
issue_body:
There is an issue with outputting type 4 addresses that are > 0xffff, they create address that have 5 digits, and are invalid in the format. i.e. have a segment with address as 0x1000e, and it will be output as two records, one the Extended Linear Address record which is correct, but then the data record has all 5 didgits of the address. example:

read and then write the following text:

:020000040001F9
:04000E00A4CFFFDD9F

you will get an error on the write due to the CRC routine getting an odd number of digits:

:020000040001F9
:041000E00A4CFFFDD9F     note address is 0x1000e

the issue is in the as_ihex function:

def as_ihex(self, size=32, address_length=32):
        """Return string of Intel HEX records of all data.

        """

        data_address = []
        extmaximumed_address = -1

        for address, data in self.segments.iter(size):
            address //= self.word_size_bytes

            if address_length == 32:
                if ((address >> 16) & 0xffff) > extmaximumed_address :
                    extmaximumed_address = ((address >> 16) & 0xffff)

#I added this fix as a hack for my situation, but would not work for subsequent segments that are in the same address space.
                    address = address & 0xffff   
#end of hack                 
                    packed = pack_ihex(4,
                                       0,
                                       2,
                                       binascii.unhexlify('%04X'
                                                          % extmaximumed_address))
                    data_address.append(packed)
            else:
                raise Error('unsupported address length %d'
                                 % address_length)

            data_address.append(pack_ihex(0, address, len(data), data))


But the function also assumes that all address are in sorted order, which I do not believe is the case for all Intel hex files, unless you are sorting them as you insert into the list, I may have missed that.

issue_title: [django] Do not generate warning for (un)needed `prefetch_related` on `get`
issue_body:
Hello.

I love the idea of this app. Wanted this/to create it for years. So, thank you.

One thing that annoy me a little is this: in the test `test_many_to_one_reverse`, `nplusone` generates a warning when selecting addresses for a user.

But I don't think it should, as doing a `prefetch_related` here won't change anything: we'll still have two queries, one to get the first user, and one to get the addresses (instantly if we use `prefetch_related`, and only when asking for addreses when not)

Sadly this prevents me to really use this lib because it can happens a lot as there is no optimization to do here, so nobody does a `prefetch_related` for a `get` (or `.first()` too?).
Think of edit forms, detail views...

The only way to change this would be to monkey patch the `get` method, to not take the current queryset into action for missing `prefetch_related` (but still do for missing `select_related`).

issue_body:
Thank you for this great tool. I'm wondering if it's reasonable to implement a feature that will allow for reading password protected encrypted credentials files for the long-term keys?

The user would implement their own encryption (e.g. gpg, openPgp, etc.) and aws-mfa would only ask for a password to decrypt in order to gain access to the long term keys (in addition to mfa).

Would this be fairly do-able? I'm also wondering if this can be done without a code change? Could I pipe the results of my decrypted long-term keys into aws-mfa somehow?

My reason for wanting this is because storing long-term access keys unencrypted on my hard disk is a security threat. I'm okay storing temporary credentials. I've already chmod'd my .aws files to 600, but still it would be nice to have them password protected in case of a phishing incident where someone was logged in as me.

Thanks again for this tool. Interested in hearing your thoughts about this added redundancy. There's a similar tool that encrypts the file too I should add: https://github.com/realestate-com-au/credulous

issue_title: Speeding up generating compressed files
issue_body:
In a project I work on, we use both `CompressedStaticFilesMixin` and the standalone compressor (`python -m whitenoise.compress <DIR>`) during Heroku deployments.

At the moment these steps are a considerable percentage (30-40%) of our deployment times.

For example using Python 2.7.13, Django 1.11.5, WhiteNoise master, a Heroku one-off performance-m dyno (2 cores, 2.5GB RAM) with the static files directory cleared (to emulate deployment, since state intentionally isn't carried over):
```
~ $ time ./manage.py collectstatic --noinput
...
156 static files copied to '/app/treeherder/static', 202 post-processed.

real    0m29.837s
user    0m29.405s
sys     0m0.359s
```

As a baseline, using the stock Django `ManifestStaticFilesStorage` results in:
```
real    0m1.031s
user    0m0.855s
sys     0m0.167s
```

For the above, the 202 files output from `ManifestStaticFilesStorage` have a combined file-size of 15MB.

Moving onto the standalone compressor (which we use on the output of a webpack build, for the SPA part of the project):
```
~ $ find dist/ -type f | wc -l
35
~ $ du -hs dist/
5.2M    dist/
~ $ time python -m whitenoise.compress dist/
...
real    0m11.929s
user    0m11.841s
sys     0m0.084s
```

Ideas off the top of my head to speed this up:
1) Use `concurrent.futures`  or similar to take advantage of all cores
2) See if the `scantree()` implementation might be faster than compress.py's `os.walk()` plus later stats
3) Reduce the number of files being compressed (eg `WHITENOISE_KEEP_ONLY_HASHED_FILES` and #147)
4) Profile both `CompressedStaticFilesMixin` and the CLI version, to double check that most of the time is indeed being spent in the compiled gzip/brotli code and not somewhere unexpected.
5) Compare the performance of the gzip stdlib and compiled brotli package with command line equivalents.

issue_title: reading then writing FT annotation gives an invalid VCF file
issue_body:
I introduced a bug when implementing the proper parsing of the FT annotation. I forgot to include code to correctly write the FT annotations. Right now, PyVCF produces an invalid VCF file when FT annotations are present. For example, this:
`GT:AD:DP:FT:GQ:PL       0:101,0:101:DP125;DP130:99:0,1800`

gets transformed to this, note the "," separating the FT annotations, instead of ";" as specified in the vcf standard:
`GT:AD:DP:FT:GQ:PL       0:101,0:101:DP125,DP130:99:0,1800`

Also, 'PASS' is left out completely when writing the vcf file:
`GT:AD:DP:FT:GQ:PL       1:0,191:191::99:7867,0`

One solution would be to add a bunch of if statements to \_format_sample to make sure the FT annotation is treated like a special case, similar to GT. On the other hand, it might be cleaner to make a full fledged class out of \_Call based on attrdict, and use \__repr\__ to write Call objects, which keeps al the edge cases like GT and FT inside the class. 
Using attrdict would also make it possible to add annotations to a call, while still being backwards compatible with the current namedtuple implementation.

issue_title: GNU Emacs terminal chokes on terminal title
issue_body:
When running rtv inside the terminal emulation of GNU Emacs[1], rtv tries to set the terminal title.  This makes no sense when running inside Emacs, but the terminal unfortunately doesn't fail gracefully.  Instead, the title is added to the first line which looks like this:

> 2;Front Page - rtv 1.18.0Front Page

While other lines are unaffected, the "cursor" is moved to the right to the beginning of the normal title (the "F" from the second occurence of "Front Page".

I've looked into it, IHMO a simple check if rtv is running inside GNU Emacs (the environment variable "INSIDE_EMACS" is set in this case) before creating the title in [page.py](https://github.com/michael-lazar/rtv/blob/master/rtv/page.py#L362) could prevent this.

Footnote:
[1]  In GNU Emacs, type 'M-x term', press return and then start rtv in the terminal.

issue_title: test
issue_body:
I thought about this while swimming :)

# Why C has const

C provides for pointers to variables (and C++ has references). This means that
variables can be affected by code far from their declaration.  I can pass a
reference to a variable into a function, and the function can operate on the
variable. The const declaration provides a way to prevent modification at a
distance.

# Why JavaScript has const

I don't know why JavaScript has const. A five-minute search didn't turn up a
good answer, just a lot of people pointing out that const is different than
immutability. I suspect const is useful to the compiler or JIT engines, since
the declaration provides information about the future that can guide their
work.

# Why Python doesn't have const

A few reasons:

1. Unlike C, Python has no way to make a reference to a name, so there's no
   risk that a function will suprisingly change a variable passed into it.

2. Unlike JavaScript, Python has no declarations that could take a const
   modifier, so there's no way to provide guidance to the compiler or JIT.

3. Because Python only has naming conventions, and no declarations enforced by
   the language, we are relying on the name to guide the developer in their
   work.  This is very different than languages with a true const declaration
   that can be enforced in whatever way the language does.


# Semantic problems with "dynamically valued constants"

It seems like `PR_NUMBER = pr['number']` is stating that we are never going to
change PR_NUMBER after this point.  Suppose I realize later that the value I
got is a string, and I'd really find an integer more convenient.  Am I allowed
to write this?

```python 
PR_NUMBER = pr['number']
PR_NUMBER = int(PR_NUMBER)
```

Seems like that would be wrong, since I am reassigning that name.  So can I
write it like this?

```python
PR_NUMBER = int(pr['number'])
```

Now I've only assigned it once, so it's a constant. But that's a very subtle
distinction between those two snippets, some might even say a trivial
distinction.  Do we really want to be switching between lower-case and
upper-case based on the number of assignment statements? It's an uninteresting
consideration.

A constant is a thing whose value never changes.  "Dynamically-valued
constants" is literally an oxymoron.

In the code in question, why was PR_NUMBER a "constant", but not other names?
By my reading of the code, we have eight other names with only a single
assignment statement (event, pr, repo, action, ignored_actions, pr_activity,
status_url, resp).   Why aren't they constants?

And then we get to the thornier issue of `result`, which is assigned in two
different places, but only one of them will be executed in any given run of the
function. Is that a constant?

Lastly, suppose we never reassign one of the parameters to a function? Should
the parameter be named with all upper-case?  That would leak internal details
to the callers of the function, and that detail could change as the function is
further developed.

Overall, it just seems to me that the number of times you assign to a name is
an uninteresting detail, unless it can help the compiler or JIT.  Naming it
with all upper-case letters won't help the compiler or JIT.


# Why does Python have constants at all?

OK, so after all that, why use upper-case constants in Python at all? They
aren't enforced, and you can still get into pedantic debates about multiple
assignments as a way to construct the value.

First, true constants, names which refer to values which are always the same,
every time you refer to them, are very useful in understanding a program.

Second, module-level or class-level constants are names that will be used
across large bodies of code, far from where they were created.  Communicating
the const-ness with a name is helpful to understanding those distant values.

In a single function, there are generally not names with truly constant values.
At the very least, that is not the kind of name in question here.

issue_title: support for config directories
issue_body:
Hello,

Thanks for writing configargparse - it is great.

It would be nice to extend the software to include arbitrary config snippets in a directory without having to specify each snippet file. Perhaps adding a named parameter to the constructor:

default_config_directories = [ '/etc/software/conf-enabled' ]
default_config_directories_globs = [ '*.conf', '*.yaml' ]

or

default config_directories = [
    { 'directory' = '/etc/software/conf-enabled', glob = '*.conf' },
]

Pros and cons to each.

Also, a way to specify the config-directory on the command line:

config_arg_parser.add(
    '--config-directory',
    is_config_directory = True,
    glob = '*.conf',
)

For larger software projects, it is nice to break the configurations into snippets. This would be a very useful feature. It would also allow mimicking a conf-available, conf-enabled paradigm.

Thanks for considering it!

-m

issue_title: Does not work with fonts that change character's shape and size depending on the word they are in.
issue_body:
I have been trying to make Urdu wordclouds. Faced the same issues as mentioned in #70 . Did workarounds mentioned. They worked but only for monospaced fonts. Or fonts that do not change the character's size, shape and placement depending on the whole word (instead of just what's immediately ahead and behind). The workaround of replacing characters with context-based unicode characters will not work in these cases. To demonstrate, I am going to write a word 'مسئلہ' (issue) in Segoe UI and Jameel Noori Nastaleeq, letter by letter.

Segoe UI first:
![issue2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26012911/32984004-babbbd4c-ccbf-11e7-8334-bc39d4623af3.png)

Using unicode (but not necessarily linguistically correct), the word is formed from 5 characters. Meem (م), Seen (س), (Yeh with) Hamza, (ئ), Laam (ل) and Heh (ہ).

1 contains meem's isolated form only. The rest contain joined beginning form of meem. Notice how all meems (coloured green) are the same. Also notice seen (coloured red). 3, 4 and 5 contain joining middle form of seen. Again, all same. However, this is not how usually Urdu (or even Arabic) is written or easily understood by native readers. Urdu uses Nastaleeq script. Arabic uses Naskh (I think). (Both of them change character's shapes. But I think it's much much less of a problem with Arabic.) Now using Nastaleeq script (i.e. Jameel Noori Nastaleeq font):

![issue](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26012911/32984082-f57008fc-ccc0-11e7-99b6-fb1ddf6b2f8a.png)

Again see meem (green) in 2-5. Although the shape is same, the height changes even though these are the same (beginning joining) form of meem. Seen (red) is also the same form in 3-5, however it's shape as well as the position changes.

These fonts do not usually explicitly contain glyphs (?) for unicode code points for explicit forms, the kind that the library arabic_reshaper gives. This is because it serves no purpose. There is no single way to display a form. (Sorry if I am a bit wrong about the technicalities.)

Some languages like Urdu, Farsi, Sindhi, Pashto, etc and a host of other languages most commonly use Nastaleeq script. Is there any way you can do something about it? It seems the underlying image processing makes decisions based on character's width and height etc. Is it possible to see how a complete word is rendered and then decision made accordingly. (Sorry if I am wrong about this. haven't yet been able to understand how it works.)

vcs import --input $CI_PROJECT_DIR/repos_file.repos $MY_WORKSPACE/src

issue_title: Equity Value calculation buggy with multi-trades
issue_body:
I just noticed that the equity value calculation is buggy with multi-trades. The problem is two fold. For stop-like calculation, it fails to correctly calculate the cash value when being short a stock. I think this is easy to test and fix. For futures instruments, the problem seems the same as the previous buggy PnL calculations. Specifically, in broker.py, right now:

  - line 245: reads cash += comminfo.cashadjust(adjsize, pprice_orig, pprice)

and

  - line 248: reads position.adjbase = pprice,

which to me should respectively be:

  - line 245: cash += comminfo.cashadjust(adjsize, position.adjbase, price)#pprice_orig, pprice) and 
  - line 248: position.adjbase = price #pprice. 

The output results with the current buggy code. Notice the wrong equity value after the second order and the wrong final equity value at the end.

issue_title: Wrong `__add__` and `__sub__` logic for FakeDate
issue_body:
I think the two functions here https://github.com/pokidovea/immobilus/blob/master/immobilus/logic.py#L76-L93 should use `date` instead of `datetime`. Otherwise we get:
```ipython
In [1]: import immobilus

In [2]: from datetime import date, timedelta

In [3]: timedelta(days=1) + date(2017, 1, 1)
Out[3]: datetime.date(2017, 1, 2)

In [4]: date(2017, 1, 1) - timedelta(days=1)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-f61d14feefaf> in <module>()
----> 1 date(2017, 1, 1) - timedelta(days=1)

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/immobilus/logic.pyc in __sub__(self, other)
     83
     84     def __sub__(self, other):
---> 85         result = datetime.__sub__(self, other)
     86
     87         if result is NotImplemented:

TypeError: descriptor '__sub__' requires a 'datetime.datetime' object but received a 'FakeDate'

In [4]: date(2017, 1, 1) + timedelta(days=1)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-11f9d8e78586> in <module>()
----> 1 date(2017, 1, 1) + timedelta(days=1)

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/immobilus/logic.pyc in __add__(self, other)
     75
     76     def __add__(self, other):
---> 77         result = datetime.__add__(self, other)
     78
     79         if result is NotImplemented:

TypeError: descriptor '__add__' requires a 'datetime.datetime' object but received a 'FakeDate'
```

By monkey patching as follows, it works as expected:
```python
import immobilus
from datetime import date

def __add__(self, other):
    result = immobilus.logic.date.__add__(self, other)

    if result is NotImplemented:
        return result

    return self.from_datetime(result)
immobilus.logic.FakeDate.__add__ = __add__

def __sub__(self, other):
    result = immobilus.logic.date.__sub__(self, other)

    if result is NotImplemented:
        return result

    if isinstance(result, date):
        return self.from_datetime(result)
    else:
        return result
immobilus.logic.FakeDate.__sub__ = __sub__
```

issue_title: Architectural changes in v1.8
issue_body:
Rollup of the top-level architecture changes in v1.8

1. Move execution logic into the test/benchmark object and *out* of the resttest.py class - they provide an execute method, and work on callbacks to log/return results (greatly simplifies all the below, quickly)
  - Allows for xUnit output: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/43
  - Allows for the macro steps to optimize dynamic evaluation, per https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/101
  - Pre-work for dynamic macros/subcomponents, from https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/92
  - Challenge: lots of command-line & test-set config options need to be consumed here. Lots of logic and dependencies on resttest.py
  - Challenge: cyclic dependency issues, need to move outputs from macro into objects too, because tests/benchmarks can't import resttest (cyclic dependency issue, since it depends on them)
2. Do some sort of mapping to unittest framework, per https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/43 and provide coupling to allow xUnit output
3. Fix the nasty parsing of configuration, per https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/75
4. Clever dynamic binding of properties/settings: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/101

Leads to option for scoped configuration in future.   If scope gets cut, steps 3 and 4 get moved to 1.9.x release, but goal is a quick execution here.

issue_title: Decrypting with ECC should not require public key
issue_body:
I don't know if this used to work or not, but I have noticed that when using ECC to decrypt it requires you to pass in the public key in addition to the private key when creating the object. I believe only the private key should be required.

It fails with

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/craig/Dropbox/codestuff/encryptr.py", line 221, in <module>
    result = new_ecc.decrypt(ciphertext)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyelliptic/ecc.py", line 535, in decrypt
    raise RuntimeError("Fail to verify data")
RuntimeError: Fail to verify data
```

Reproduce code:

```python
import pyelliptic

ecc = pyelliptic.ECC(curve='prime256v1')
public_key = ecc.get_pubkey()
private_key = ecc.get_privkey()
plaintext = 'abc'
ciphertext = ecc.encrypt(plaintext, public_key)

# this works fine
new_ecc = pyelliptic.ECC(privkey=private_key, pubkey=public_key, curve='prime256v1')
result = new_ecc.decrypt(ciphertext)

# this raises exception
new_ecc = pyelliptic.ECC(privkey=private_key, curve='prime256v1')
result = new_ecc.decrypt(ciphertext)
```

issue_body:
I'm running Hypothesis in a Docker container. On the first run, everything works fine. One the second run I get this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
[…]
  File "[my_test].py", line 18, in <module>
    import hypothesis.strategies as st
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/strategies.py", line 71, in <module>
    @defines_strategy
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/strategies.py", line 51, in defines_strategy
    @proxies(strategy_definition)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/internal/reflection.py", line 497, in accept
    copy_argspec(target.__name__, getargspec(target))(proxy)))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/internal/reflection.py", line 465, in accept
    u'invocation': u', '.join(invocation_parts)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/hypothesis/internal/reflection.py", line 372, in source_exec_as_module
    f = open(temporary_filepath, u'w')
IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: u'/.hypothesis/eval_source/hypothesis_temporary_module_9abd2800900986980ad8f68a32f0005fc05800d8_53a57e8c-7a3a-4e9a-9a0c-8a8641d521d4.py'
```

The immediate cause of the `IOError` appears to be that Hypothesis tries to create a directory directly beneath `/`. This has to do with my setup, so I don't blame you. My concern is that Hypothesis tries to create a directory somewhere in the file system on module load. This strikes me as unorthodox.

Specifically, [`hypothesis_home_dir()`](https://github.com/DRMacIver/hypothesis/blob/v1.13.0/src/hypothesis/settings.py#L51) creates a `.hypothesis` directory either under a directory specified by the undocumented environment variable `HYPOTHESIS_STORAGE_DIRECTORY` or under the current working directory. The latter case is what ultimately causes the above exception. The former case I now use to redirect the `.hypothesis` creation to some other place. This, however, is a nasty workaround.

测试后发现`memberList/chatroomList`一直都是空的，于是把`client.py`修改了一下

测试后可行。

另外判断是否来自公众号我的方法是先取得公众号的列表，之后对发来消息的ID通过是否在已知列表里进行判断。于是我参照memberList和chatroomList增加了publicList这个项，在get_contract()这个函数里和另外两个list一道进行分类。

关于在获取到的list进行普通/群/公众号的分类的过程中，我发现源代码是复制出N个数组，分别把不属于该组的数据remove掉，这就要进行N次遍历，我觉得有点低效，就把这几次遍历写到一起了，get_contract()的代码：

```python
def get_contract(self, update=False):
    if 1 < len(self.memberList) and not update:
        return self.memberList
    url = '%s/webwxgetcontact?r=%s&seq=0&skey=%s' % (self.loginInfo['url'],
                                                     int(time.time()),
                                                     self.loginInfo['skey'])
    headers = {
        'ContentType': 'application/json; charset=UTF-8'
    }
    r = self.s.get(url, headers=headers)
    memberList = json.loads(BytesDecode(r.content.decode('utf-8', 'replace')))['MemberList']
    chatroomList = []
    publicList = []
    while True:
        i = 0
        for m in memberList:
            if (any([str(n) in m['UserName'] for n in range(10)])
                and any([
                    [chr(n) in m['UserName'] for n in range(ord('a'), ord('z') + 1)],
                    [chr(n) in m['UserName'] for n in range(ord('A'), ord('Z') + 1)]
                ])):
                if ('@@' in m['UserName']):
                    chatroomList.append(m)
                elif (m['Sex'] == 0
                      and (m['VerifyFlag'] & 8 != 0)):
                    publicList.append(m)
                elif (m['Sex'] != 0
                      or (m['VerifyFlag'] & 8 == 0)):
                    continue
            memberList.remove(m)
            i += 1
        if i == 0:
            break
    # deal with emoji
    self.memberList = tools.emoji_dealer(memberList)
    self.publicList = publicList
    self.chatroomList = chatroomList
    return memberList
```

issue_title: Unpin dependencies
issue_body:
Currently the setup.py includes a requirements.txt, which requires specific versions of every requirement. This causes problems when combined with other software since they could require a version range which isn't compatible with the specific version required here.

Or consider another scenario: I need a newer version of e.g. requests, then I'm stuck because this package is pinned to an older version.

This article goes into a bit more depth about why setup.py should be treated differently to requirements.txt https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/

My suggestion is that the dependencies should be moved into setup.py and the version should be changed to minimum versions (using >=) rather than exact version requirements.

issue_title: CronTrigger Constructor: 'str' object has no attribute 'tzinfo'
issue_body:
In the docs for `CronTrigger`, it says `start_date` and `end_date` can be passed as `datetime` or `str` values. But when you attempt to pass a string to the constructor:

```python
trigger = CronTrigger(year='*', month='*/2', week='*', day='1',
    day_of_week='*', hour='0', minute='0', second='0',
    start_date='2016-11-01 12:15:00')
```

You end up with:

```python
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/apscheduler/triggers/cron/__init__.py", line 51, in __init__
    elif start_date and start_date.tzinfo:
AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'tzinfo'
```

Which makes sense because a string does indeed not have a `tzinfo` attribute. There should probably be a check to see if `start_date` is a `datetime` before checking if it has a `tzinfo` attribute.

The easiest workaround is probably to pass in the local timezone in via `timezone`.

Note: I'm constructing a trigger instance manually here because my application needs to be compiled using PyInstaller, and correct me if I'm wrong, but this is the solution you suggested [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!searchin/apscheduler/lookuperror/apscheduler/OR3MSBHwd8Q/0BzP3xpuGmAJ).

issue_title: Delay on Linux due to \r vs \n ?
issue_body:
When I send a command to Onkyo, the response time is several seconds. Until today, I thought that the receiver is just slow, but I just made an observation that might tell a different story:

Playing around with an ESP8266, I implemented a client that talks to a Linux host. The line where it reads back from the host is: `String reply = client.readStringUntil('\r');` The same function is used to implement a server on embedded systems.

The observation is that the line always blocks one second. The Linux Host sends the reply immediately, but my ESP8266 woud wait another second before the above line terminates. Turns out that the Linux Host never sends `\r` (`\r\n` is a Windows thing) but a `\n` instead and the default timeout for `readStringUntil` is 1000ms. Changing the `\r` to `\n` caused the device to reply immediately because it didn't wait for the timeout any more.

Could it be that the slow Onkyo isn't that slow in reality? On the app, the reaction is immediate. Could it be that onkyo-eiscp sends just `\n` if it is run on Linux hosts but the Onkyo expects `\r` ?

issue_title: Unnumbered figures have numbers in pdf output
issue_body:
I'm declaring this as an issue but will not likely address it until there is demand for a fix.

There are three different labeling possibilities arising from the syntax [proposed](https://github.com/jgm/pandoc/issues/813#issuecomment-70423503) by @scaramouche1 in pandoc Issue #813.

 1) Numbered, referenceable figures:

        ![Caption.](fig.png){#fig:A}

 2) Numbered, unreferenceable figures:

        ![Caption.](fig.png){#fig:}

 3) Unnumbered, unreferenceable figures:

        ![Caption.](fig.png){}

Presently, pandoc-fignos does the right thing for html output.  For PDF output, however, the case 3 figures are numbered.  This is because pandoc's TeX writer only produces numbered figures.

Notes:

  * A possible workaround in pandoc-fignos: Disable automatic caption numbering in LaTeX altogether and hard-code it as is done for the other output formats.  Not pretty, but it could be used in a pinch.

  * LaTeX allows unnumbered figures by using the `\caption*` command in the [caption](https://www.ctan.org/tex-archive/macros/latex/contrib/caption/) package.

Observing an error with 1.2.11 that does not occur with 1.2.10

issue_title: Parametric Weighting factors
issue_body:
Docs show the CIEDE2000 takes Kl, Kc, and Kh as args, all set at unity to 1, which does not seem to be required. 

`delta_e_cie2000(color1, color2, Kl=1, Kc=1, Kh=1)`

In the Sharma, Wu and Dalal paper, these parametric weighting factors are used in equation 22, in the denominators. kl is used once, while kc and kh are used twice. While this would be a big deal if anything other than 1 were used, that is not the case in the application here, and the constancy of the weighting factors makes passing them in as agruments useless.

issue_title: Missing Key-Usage and Subkey-Usage in generation key input
issue_body:
For the moment, we can't generate a RSA key for encryption and/or authentication :
```
>>> import gnupg
>>> gpg = gnupg.GPG()
>>> print(gpg.gen_key_input(key_type="RSA",key_usage="encrypt,auth"))
Key-Type: RSA
Key-Length: 4096
Expire-Date: 2017-10-10
Name-Email: lucien@sangohan
Name-Real: Autogenerated Key
%commit
```

Here is a part of gnupg.py, in gen_key_input
```python
for key, val in list(kwargs.items()):
    key = key.replace('_','-').title()
    ## to set 'cert', 'Key-Usage' must be blank string
    if not key in ('Key-Usage', 'Subkey-Usage'):
        if type('')(val).strip():
            parms[key] = val
```
It adds all arguments to parms, except the Key-Usage and Subkey-Usage.
Why is that ?
Moreover, the comment is in contradiction with the docstring :
```
:param str key_usage: Space or comma delimited string of key
    usages. Allowed values are ‘encrypt’, ‘sign’, and ‘auth’. This is
    used to generate the key flags. Please make sure that the
    algorithm is capable of this usage. Note that OpenPGP requires
    that all primary keys are capable of certification, so no matter
    what usage is given here, the ‘cert’ flag will be on. If no
    ‘Key-Usage’ is specified and the ‘Key-Type’ is not ‘default’, all
    allowed usages for that particular algorithm are used; if it is
    not given but ‘default’ is used the usage will be ‘sign’.
```

I am getting an error:

```
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xd0 in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
  File "C:\Users\Mick\Desktop\Projects\django\meg-forms\lib\site-packages\django\core\mail\message.py", line 348, in send
    return self.get_connection(fail_silently).send_messages([self])

```

Relevant code: 
```python
attachment.filename = filename
# todo: Read content if stream?
if isinstance(content, str):
    # line 161:

attachment.content = base64.b64encode(content).decode()
attachment.type = mimetype
```
the attachment is an xls document and the first byte us `0xd0`. This crashes sendgrid, because `b'\xd0'.encode()` produces an error. issue_title: Suggestion: only remove files older than <some period of time>
issue_body:
With large databases and many files there can be a delay between getting the used files from the database and all the files from disk. Inbetween a file may have been uploaded, which will then be deleted by the script. While this is an edge case and not very likely to happen, I would suggest adding an option (or making it default) to only remove files that have not been modified for some length of time, for example a couple of minutes or an hour.

If you like this idea I'll be happy to prepare a pull request adding this functionality, but before I do that I am wondering what your thoughts are.

issue_title: Hello I want image path when file select and show it in controller.js.
Maybe move away from patching over "extra" extensions
issue_body: Currently, pymdown extensions allows you to include *pymdown-extensions* with default *extra* extensions. Maybe a less annoying approach would be to have a *pymdown-extra* extension that stands in place of *extra* and does things like replace smartstrong with betterem etc.  This way we can move away from the post extension load hack where we overwrite smartstrong etc.  Instead we can just educate the user on what extensions are incompatible and provide them with a drop in replacement.  For instance, you shouldn't use smartstrong and betterem together; just pick one.

issue_title: create_replication source format
document.update_field() raises 409 after successful retry
issue_body:
## Bug Description

### 1. Steps to reproduce and the simplest code sample possible to demonstrate the issue

The `Document.update_field()` method raises an HTTPError(409) exception if it succeeds with its retries without exhausting the max retries.

Here is the code in `Document._update_field()` (see [here](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/document.py#L262)):

```
        # Attempt to save, retrying conflicts up to max_tries.
        try:
            self.save()
        except requests.HTTPError as ex:
            if tries < max_tries and ex.response.status_code == 409:
                self._update_field(
                    action, field, value, max_tries, tries=tries+1)
            raise
```

The `raise` statement should be in an `else:` clause if the previous `if` statement.

If you need a script that reproduces this, let me know, but it is too long to paste here (I think), and from looking at the code, the issue should be obvious.

### 2. What you expected to happen

The `Document.update_field()` method should not raise an HTTPError(409) exception if it ended up with a successful retry without exhausting its max retries. Only if the max retries are exhausted, should it raise the exception.

The description of the `Document.update_field()` method should document that.

There should be test cases that verify that case. :-)

### 3. What actually happened

See above.

## Environment details

- Version(s) that are affected by this issue.
    > 2.9.0
- Python version
    > 2.7.14

issue_title: Scheduler can't restart until long-running local executor(s) finish
issue_body:
Dear Airflow Maintainers,

Before I tell you about my issue, let me describe my environment:

### Environment

* Version of Airflow (e.g. a release version, running your own fork, running off master -- provide a git log snippet): 1.7.0
* Airflow components and configuration, if applicable (e.g. "Running a Scheduler with CeleryExecutor")
LocalExecutor, set parallelism = 128
* Example code to reproduce the bug (as a code snippet in markdown)
* Screen shots of your DAG's graph and tree views: 
* Stack trace if applicable:
* Operating System: (Windows Version or `$ `): Linux blizzard-flowrida 3.16.0-4-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 3.16.7-ckt20-1+deb8u3 (2016-01-17) x86_64 GNU/Linux
* Python Version: `$ python --version`: 2.7.9

Now that you know a little about me, let me tell you about the issue I am having:

### Description of Issue

* What did you expect to happen?

When scheduler runs out of num_runs (we set to 200), it should force local executor's running task to stop. (e.g sends a kill signal). 

* What happened instead?

The current implementation waits until each executor drains its queue. However, if we have long-running tasks, e.g sensor_task to detect a file exists, scheduler would simply wait there, doing nothing.

I'm not listing any code here because I feel it's pretty clear what the issue is, can you please help look into it or suggest a workaround? Thanks a lot!

issue_title: Use the new Roboto
issue_body:
The Roboto version shipped with Barceloneta is the old Roboto. In 2014 a new, improved Roboto was released. Should we ship the new one?

http://www.fontsquirrel.com/fonts/roboto
http://www.fontsquirrel.com/fonts/roboto-2014

Old:
------
![screen shot 2015-12-27 at 20 32 01](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1969342/12025842/187b3828-adb3-11e5-839d-bb22f8a0ee19.png)
New:
------
![screen shot 2015-12-27 at 20 33 27](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1969342/12025843/187b982c-adb3-11e5-86fd-7b0ee4d4e2f4.png)

Difference of the type faces used in above screenshots:
- The old `K` has a typical horizontal bar. The new Roboto uses a more traditional (and less dissonant) glyph shape.
- The ascenders and descenders of the new Roboto are smaller. Or the x height is relatively increased.  This is considered more legible.
- The italics seem more balanced, same greyness as the regular text. 
- The new Roboto runs more economically. Glyphs have less width. (Most obvious in line length of footer text and the wrapping of the text in the left portlet)
- `<` and `>` and bullets have better horizontal alignment.

I think the new Roboto is more legible and better balanced and should be supplied with Barceloneta.

"put" not available for dns_records

Hi,

the readme states that put is available for `dns_records`-operations:

GET | PUT | POST | PATCH | DELETE | API call
-- | -- | -- | -- | -- | --
(...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...)
`GET` | `PUT` | `POST` |   | `DELETE` | /zones/:identifier/dns_records
(...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...)

However when using `dns_records.put()` (`cf.zones.dns_records.put(zone_id, data=record)`) the SDK throws an error: `CloudFlare.exceptions.CloudFlareAPIError: Method PUT not available for that URI.`

At least the readme should be correct about the available methods, but ideally an idempotent `PUT` should be available for `dns_records` to avoid duplicate records.

issue_title: Improve the dbserver behaviour when CTRL-C is fired
issue_body:
When the _dbserver_ process is started manually (usually by `oq dbserver start`) it cannot be stopped with `ctrl-c`. If `ctrl-c` is pressed by mistake the process does not exit even if `oq dbserver stop` says "dbserver already stopped" and the _dbserver_ process can be stopped only using kill; this is dangerous because even if the process stays alive it does not accept any new connection and another _dbserver_ process is spawned if a new calculation is fired.

A similar issue appears when `oq webui start` is used: when `ctrl-c` is fired to stop the _webui server_ the dbserver stays in background but it cannot be stopped by `oq dbserver stop` (it returns "dbserver already stopped") and must be killed instead. As said before if a new calculation is started a new dbserver process is created:

```bash
$ oq webui start

issue_title: Enable snapshot copy with custom KMS master keys
issue_body:
This is a feature request to enable support for using custom KMS keys as part of the snapshot copy function.  It will handle 2 conditions:
1. Allow a copy from an un-encrypted snapshot to an encrypted snapshot with a custom key.
2. Allow a copy from an encrypted snapshot with a custom key to a new snapshot with a different custom key.

Here is the AWS release statement...
On 7/3, AWS  announced two enhancements to EBS Snapshot copy, which address two common Enterprise customers requests.
The first enhancement allows you to copy encrypted snapshots across regions within your account using custom KMS CMKs. Previously, you could copy only unencrypted snapshots and encrypted snapshots using your default CMK for EBS. This feature allows you to increase reliability with geographical redundancy, while also meeting your compliance needs. The second enhancement allows you to create an encrypted copy of an unencrypted snapshot.  These enhancements also allow us to deliver even more features later this year, such as encrypted cross-account snapshot copy and encrypted boot volumes.

issue_title: Creating Resource Pool Results in 0 Resource Capacity on New Pool
issue_body:
Running CreateResourcePool() on an existing vim.ResourcePool object works successfully, however I stumbled upon not being able to start any virtual machines in resource pools I create with this method. Manually creating the pools with the same settings in vsphere web client results in the desired behavior. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7663028/31414315-946b9666-adda-11e7-8d55-7773299336b0.png)
As you can see, this resource pool has 0 available resources in either capacity or free space. I have to manually re-create this respool to fix the issue. 
This is the function I wrote to create a resource pool:
```python
def create_student_respool(username):
    content = si.RetrieveContent()
    computeConsumer = get_role(content, "Compute Consumer")
    users = get_obj(content, [vim.ResourcePool], "users")
    configSpec = vim.ResourceConfigSpec()
    sharesInfo = vim.SharesInfo(level='normal')
    cpuAllocationInfo = vim.ResourceAllocationInfo()
    memAllocationInfo = vim.ResourceAllocationInfo()
    
    cpuAllocationInfo.reservation = 0
    cpuAllocationInfo.expandableReservation = False
    cpuAllocationInfo.limit = 20000  # 20GHz
    cpuAllocationInfo.shares = sharesInfo
    
    
    memAllocationInfo.reservation = 0
    memAllocationInfo.expandableReservation = False
    memAllocationInfo.limit = 8192  # 8GB
    memAllocationInfo.shares = sharesInfo
    
    configSpec.cpuAllocation = cpuAllocationInfo
    configSpec.memoryAllocation = memAllocationInfo
    new_pool = users.CreateResourcePool(username, configSpec)
    new_perms = [
        vim.AuthorizationManager.Permission(
            entity=new_pool,
            principal="CRL\\{}".format(username),
            group = False, 
            roleId = computeConsumer.roleId, 
            propagate=True
        )
    ]
    content.authorizationManager.SetEntityPermissions(new_pool, new_perms)
    return new_pool
```
I'm also setting permissions on the resource pool using my own predefined role and a user principal name that corresponds to an Active Directory user that I specify.

Am I missing a field in the configSpec? or does this look like a bug?

issue_title: Add "label=" locator strategy
issue_body:
I would like to have another locator strategy added, tentatively named "label". This would look for a label  element with the given text, then return the element pointed to by the "for" attribute of that label element.

Rationale:

Django (and other frameworks?) create forms that look like this:

```
<label for="id_some_form_field">I accept the terms</label>
<input id='id_some_form_field' type='checkbox'>
```

I would like to interact with the form field by specifying the label rather than having to use some other locator. For example, to validate that this checkbox is checked I want to do something like this:

```
click element    label=I accept the terms
checkbox should be selected    label=I accept the terms
```

I think this would make it easier for people who didn't write the code be able to write tests, because they wouldn't have to know the underlying element IDs; instead, they can simply enter in what they see on the screen.

Further, I recommend we support the stripping of leading and trailing spaces and dots to allow for a simple type of pattern matching. Say, for example, you have a form field that has the label "I have read the terms of service and agree to be bound by them". It would be cumbersome to type all of that into a test case, so it would be nice to support something like this:

```
checkbox should be selected    label=I have read the terms of service...
```

I am willing to write the implementation. This issue is for generating discussion, and to see if this would be a generally useful locator strategy.

issue_title: pipenv shell should have an option to stay in the same directory
issue_body:
##### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

In my django projects, the manage.py isn't in the root of the virtualenv, so a common pattern before  I used pipenv was this:
```
cd /project/django
source ../.venv/bin/activate
```
Which meant I stayed in `/project/django`
However, with `pipenv shell`:
```
cd /project/django
pipenv shell
```
The new directory is `/project`

##### Describe the solution you'd like

Either pipenv shell should stay in the same directory after using pipenv shell to enter a virtualenv by default or the command should have an option to indicate this behavior.

##### Describe alternatives you've considered

Changing the project root or just using the `source` command.

issue_title: Allow option to suppress info messages that are sent to stderr
issue_body:
As per https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/628 and https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/pull/630, some output, e.g. courtesy notices and messages like  
> Loading .env environment variables…

are sent to stderr instead of stdout. This is somewhat unexpected behaviour as these are clearly not error messages. In fact, these messages are polluting some of our error logs. It would be really useful to have an option to completely suppress these messages, e.g. by allowing the `--bare` flag for the `run` and `shell` commands.

issue_title: .env does not take precedence over .bashrc
issue_body:
If I set a particular environment variable (in this case `ORACLE_HOME`) in both my `.bashrc` and the project's `.env`, the `.bashrc` value will take precedence, even inside `pipenv shell`. (Apparently `.env` doesn't even get run with `pipenv install`?)

------------

##### Expected result

The value of an environmental variable set in a project's `.env` file would take precedence over the value of the same variable set in the user's `.bashrc`.

##### Actual result

The value of an environmental variable set in the user's `.bashrc` file takes precedence over the value of the same variable set in a project's `.env`.

##### Steps to replicate

# Set `ORACLE_HOME` to one value in your user's `.bashrc` (probably using `export`).
# Set `ORACLE_HOME` to another value in your project's `.env`.
# Run `pipenv run echo $ORACLE_HOME` and note the value.

issue_title: test command does not return failure code

I tracked the change of behavior to 53b47e1dfa9dfb1e8b94172a4650409fc03d4048

About #850, I think it is an expected behavior that if a command in a list fails that the following are not executed. So I think the revision should be reverted.

issue_title: ruaml.yaml.load raises warning
issue_body:
Hi. It looks like the last version of ruamel.yaml raises a warning when calling `load()`, and suggest using `safe_load` to avoid arbitrary code execution via configuration files. Here is a sample stacktrace from a project of mine importing `dateparser`

```
    /opt/unblock_rule/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dateparser/conf.py:47: UnsafeLoaderWarning: 
    The default 'Loader' for 'load(stream)' without further arguments can be unsafe.
    Use 'load(stream, Loader=ruamel.yaml.Loader)' explicitly if that is OK.
    Alternatively include the following in your code:
    
      import warnings
      warnings.simplefilter('ignore', ruamel.yaml.error.UnsafeLoaderWarning)
    
    In most other cases you should consider using 'safe_load(stream)'
      cls._yaml_data = load_yaml(data).pop('settings', {})
    /opt/unblock_rule/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dateparser/languages/loader.py:46: UnsafeLoaderWarning: 
    The default 'Loader' for 'load(stream)' without further arguments can be unsafe.
    Use 'load(stream, Loader=ruamel.yaml.Loader)' explicitly if that is OK.
    Alternatively include the following in your code:
    
      import warnings
      warnings.simplefilter('ignore', ruamel.yaml.error.UnsafeLoaderWarning)
    
    In most other cases you should consider using 'safe_load(stream)'
      data = yaml.load(data)

```

I suggest replacing all occurence of `yaml.load()` by `yaml.safe_load()`

issue_title: Input Issue Resulting in Infinite Loop
issue_body:
On extremely rare occasions, (3 in ~16000 for my own work), the fitting routine in photutils.isophote.fitter results in an infinite loop. My own code that uses the fitting routine is below.
```
flux = Ellipse(data)  # Where data is a valid numpy ndarray with no nan or inf pixels

try:
    fitting_list = flux.fit_image()
except IndexError:
    fitting_list = []
...
```

When this infinite loop occurs, it continuously logs the following:
```
INFO: Improper input: N=5 must not exceed M=0 [photutils.isophote.fitter]
INFO: Improper input: N=5 must not exceed M=0 [photutils.isophote.fitter]
...
```
which appears to be a result of the code on lines 151-155. Perhaps changing the way this exception is handled to a warning would be a better alternative?

issue_title: Module docstring on top of documentation
issue_body:
Could not find an open issue on this, but its related to #880.

There is some [fine documentation](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/viewer/widgets/core.py#L1-17) laying around which is not included in the doc build (as far as I can see).

Currently, the skimage module has the docstring [included](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/api/skimage.html) and I guess this is because it's included in `__init__.py`.

A simple fix would be to move the docstrings over to `__init__.py` as `module.core/base` isn't intended to use for imports anyhow. Disadvantage would be to those who browse the source, but I guess they will find `__init__.py` too.

If this is too much hassle, it might be possible with some single line `__doc__`-wizardry?

issue_title: Incompatibility clVerbosity / verbosity for classifiers
issue_body:
The parameter dict in classifiers (SDR and CLA) uses `clVerbosity` as parameter name for verbose level, and the actual parameter name is `verbosity`. This is fine in principle but the getter methods for Regions use this param dict as reference for valid parameter names, and thus are expecting `verbosity`.

Thus a stable fix would be to use verbosity instead of `clVerbosity` in those `param` dicts.

issue_title: Travis CI configuration should specify image version
issue_body: In investigating OS X Travis builds, I found https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/languages/objective-c/#Supported-Xcode-versions, which lists several different images to be used.  One theory about the current breakage seems to point to recent changes in the travis environment that we haven't been able to track down, exactly.  Once we get a stable OS X build, we should specify the `osx_image` configuration directive so that when the default changes, it doesn't immediately break builds.  Instead, when the default changes, we should follow up with a PR to change it and if it breaks, then include any additional changes necessary to work as part of that PR.

issue_title: Borrower notification templates are not easily extensible
issue_labels: [{'id': 96304291, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Branch:%20maint-1.1', 'name': 'Branch: maint-1.1', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': False}, {'id': 96240745, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Component:%20BibCirculation', 'name': 'Component: BibCirculation', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 414883170, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Size:%20beginner-friendly', 'name': 'Size: beginner-friendly', 'color': '8646c6', 'default': False}, {'id': 96238448, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'Type: enhancement', 'color': 'ff5500', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Basically, due to [load_template()](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/d4654c31b75c6445eeb6daf04cd8b8755ce70e5a/modules/bibcirculation/lib/bibcirculationadminlib.py#L290) implementation in `bibcirculationadminlib` it is currently not possible to add additional borrower notification forms without changing invenio core code. As the templates available are configured via the `CFG_BIBCIRCULATION_TEMPLATES`-dictionary, it seems reasonable to allow all keys in this very dict as valid templates.

Thus, the minimal invasive, invisible, change to the code would be to add a further `elif` statement checking if value passed is a key in the dict and if so just load the template associated. If one wants to restrict templates displayed to the librarian one could then move this kind of config to the `bibcirculation_template_<CFG_WEBSTYLE_TEMPLATE_SKIN>` for the generation of the associated select-box in html.

isRequiredFor: join2/join2#1066, join2/join2-data#2011

issue_title: tests: simplify doctest execution
issue_labels: [{'id': 414886429, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-config/labels/Size:%20easy', 'name': 'Size: easy', 'color': 'bc3cbc', 'default': False}, {'id': 414886377, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-config/labels/Status:%20ready%20for%20work', 'name': 'Status: ready for work', 'color': '149414', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: 100% coverage
Review Base_getattro implementation for Python 3.7 compatibility
issue_body:
Python 3.7 is introducing a number of new OP codes to speed up method calling. I don't understand all the details, but `object.c` has a new `_PyObject_GetMethod` method, which is doing almost the same as `_PyObject_GenericGetAttrWithDict`, which is the basis for our customized `Base_getattro`.

The implementation on the Python side is spread out over a couple of issues, the two most relevant I could find are http://bugs.python.org/issue26110 and http://bugs.python.org/issue29263, with the initial patch adding the new method in https://hg.python.org/cpython/rev/64afd5cab40a.

Before we can support Python 3.7, someone should have a detailed look at this. Hopefully if we do nothing everything continues to work. But there might be an opportunity to opt-into the new feature and benefit from the new op code optimizations for classes deriving from ExtensionClass.

issue_title: "Allow USB debugging?" dialog show every time after calling adb_commands.AdbCommands.ConnectDevice
issue_body:
OS: Windows 7 x64
Branch: Master
Phone: Redmi Note 2
Android version: 5.0.2

I forked and use cryptography.io instead of M2Crypto to do the RSA thing in sign_m2crypto, because installing M2Crypto on Windows is painful and I can't manage it :(
Everything works fine, except the "Allow USB debugging?" dialog pops up every time I call adb_commands.AdbCommands.ConnectDevice, even after I check the "Always allow from this computer" checkbox.
Is this an issue or a designed behavior?

Here's my cryptography signer
```python
class CryptographySigner(adb_protocol.AuthSigner):

  def __init__(self, rsa_key_path):
    with open(rsa_key_path + '.pub') as rsa_pub_file:
      self.public_key = rsa_pub_file.read()

    with open(rsa_key_path, 'rb') as key_file:
      self.rsa_key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(
        key_file.read(),
        password=None,
        backend=default_backend()
      )
  
  def Sign(self, data):
    return self.rsa_key.sign(
      data,
      padding.PKCS1v15(),
      hashes.SHA1()
    )

  def GetPublicKey(self):
    return self.public_key
```

Here is my test script
```python
from os import path

from adb import adb_commands
from adb import sign_m2crypto
from adb import sign_pythonrsa

signer = sign_m2crypto.CryptographySigner(
  path.expanduser('~/.android/adbkey')
)

device = adb_commands.AdbCommands.ConnectDevice(
  auth_timeout_ms=3000,   # confirm manually on device
  rsa_keys=[signer]
)

for i in xrange(10):
  print device.Shell('echo %d' % i)

print device.Shell('ls')
```

issue_title: Conda install of galsim
issue_body:
Is it now possible to use the LSST conda channels to perform a conda installation of GalSim that does not pull in a lot of unrelated LSST packages?  That would be great, so this issue is to document the current status and hopefully get it working.

As a first test, I created a conda environment containing only the LSST galsim package:

```
% conda create -n galsim14 --channel http://conda.lsst.codes/sims --channel defaults --override-channels lsst-galsim
```

This looks promising:

```
% source activate galsim14
(galsim14) % conda --list
# packages in environment at /Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14:
#
astropy                   1.2.1               np111py27_0

lsst-astropy-eups-configs 0.0.1.2                       0    http://conda.lsst.codes/sims

lsst-python-d2to1         0.2.12.1                      0    http://conda.lsst.codes/sims

Note that I named my env `galsim14` expecting to get the new version 1.4 but I actually got 1.3.x. Also note that the LSST stack uses nomkl versions of numpy, etc, but that is hopefully a temporary issue (more details [here](https://github.com/DarkEnergyScienceCollaboration/WeakLensingDeblending/issues/17#issuecomment-239213187)).

Unfortunately, I cannot `import galsim` in this new environment.  My first test using ipython failed because I did not install ipython in the new environment (which is easy to fix), so ipython ran in my conda root environment and picked up my previously non-conda-installed galsim.

My second test used the plain python shell, so runs within the new environment, but now cannot find galsim.  My module search path looks ok:

```
>>> import sys ; sys.path

'/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/lib/python2.7',

but it looks like galsim is installed under `/Users/david/anaconda/envs/galsim14/opt/lsst/galsim` which is not being search.  Perhaps some EUPS magic is required here?

issue_title: Add a "host" option to openfisca-serve
issue_body:
When using the `openfisca-serve` script, I can specify a port but not a host to bind to; the script defaults to `localhost` which is appropriate only in a narrow range of conditions. It will for instance prevent usage from a Docker container, a throwaway virtual machine on a cloud provider, or that home NAS that you use as a server for tinkering with stuff.

Possibly a better default is `0.0.0.0`, a pseudo-host which will result in the server socket binding on every available interface. Alternately, a config option `--host` to override `localhost` is perfectly suitable.

issue_title: job.py in bigquery does unnecessary client-side validation of data formats
issue_body:
The BigQuery engineering team may decide to expand the allowed formats for import and export, but the allowed values are hard-coded client side. Imports are limited to CSV, DATASTORE_BACKUP, NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON, AVRO, for example.

This means that if the BigQuery starts to allow other formats, users will be unable to use those new formats without updating their client. Moreover, there is no way for BigQuery to roll out new formats to a whitelisted set of users. 
(note this is not mere idle speculation and is currently impacting real users alpha testing a new format)

See code here:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/bigquery/google/cloud/bigquery/job.py#L116
and 
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/bigquery/google/cloud/bigquery/job.py#L139

issue_title: Is it recommended to use multiprocess with gevent's socket patch?
issue_body:
Hi,

I am working on a non-blocking I/O gevent-powered web application that is required to launch a child process to handle a CPU-intensive task which can be crashed randomly because of bugs in the underlying C/C++ code.

However, Python's standard multiprocessing has defined its own socket that makes it incompatible with gevent's patch. As a result, the following code does not work

```
from gevent.monkey import patch_all
patch_all()
from multiprocessing import Process, Pipe

def test_document(conn, local_path):
    try:
        file_utils.test_document(local_path)
    except BaseException as e:
        conn.send(e)
    else:
        conn.send("done")
    conn.close()

parent_conn, child_conn = Pipe()
p = Process(target=test_document, args=(child_conn, local_path,))
p.start()
try:
    message = None
    # Broken because of gevent
    message = parent_conn.recv() # An IOError (Resource temporarily unavailable) will be thrown
except:
    # Always get a IOError, message will be never returned
   pass
```

The solution to this issue is to unpatch it which makes multiprocessing code work.However, it forces my application to be blocked on I/O again.

What about `multiprocess`? Can it handle the issue or I need to implement a work-around?

Thanks

issue_title: [pantsd] don't fingerprint config-files or rc-files
issue_body: Stu had a great thought earlier which was essentially that in order to avoid daemon lifecycle invalidation when the string values of  `--pants-config-files` or `--pantsrc-files` change, we should not fingerprint these (because their transitive inner values will already be considered during fingerprinting and may or may not be affected by pointing to a new config or rc filename).

issue_title: GoRemoteLibrary/GoFetch do not support packages in subdirectories.
issue_body:
Some repos place all their code in one or more subpackages and this trips up GoFetch recusrsive resolution.  Concretely, for a github repo with go code laid out like so:
```
https://github.com/pantsbuild/golibs/
  rpc/
    rpc.go
  io/
    io.go
  util/
    dir.go
    file.go
```
Will fail [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/tasks/go_fetch.py#L201-L202) when GoFetch tries to determine if the repo has its own remote dependencies to fetch.  In general GoFetch needs to recursively glob all go files in the exploded zip package dir.

An example of this sort of repo is here: https://github.com/AdRoll/goamz

issue_title: Support invalidation generically via an option
issue_body:
Currently, manual invalidation is only supported via `./pants invalidated`. This is problematic for a few reasons:
1. "invalidate" is not a verb in pants user-space, it's only one we use internally in the codebase
2. The `invalidate` goal acts globally, invalidating all targets across all tasks

As @benjyw suggested [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/4660#pullrequestreview-43801196), a global recursive `--force` flag might take the place of the `invalidate` goal and also allow more targeted invalidation at the task level when placed to the right of a fully qualified goal name on the command line. To-boot, `--force` likely make more intuitive sense in pants user-space; ie: force this command to run instead of skipping things.

issue_title: "Missing BUILD dependency" messages look like errors, but are treated as a warning
issue_body:
Currently, pants emits errors such as this:

```
Missing BUILD dependency finagle/finagle-thrift/src/test/java -> .pants.d/compile/java/classes/com/twitter/finagle/thrift/ThriftClientRequest.class
```

When it encounters a BUILD file with a missing dependency. These messages are printed in red, and look like errors. However, they do not cause the pants build to fail, and thus in practice act more like warnings.

We could either promote these messages to become actual errors, or we could treat this as a knob, that users could tweak (sort of like how gcc can promote warnings to error via a flag).

issue_title: Mandatory 'nomonitor' breaks souschef
issue_body:
* ricecooker version: 0.6.16 (was fine in .13, I think)
* Python version: 3.5.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04

### Description

I tried to run an existing souschef (https://github.com/learningequality/sushi-chef-artsedge) and after upgrading it gave a KeyError for 'nomonitor' on https://github.com/learningequality/ricecooker/blob/f75986ac2681b64dee74697eb2863c4db05cb30c/ricecooker/commands.py#L110

This leads to calling `.run` on a `SushiChef` instance to crash if no `nomonitor` attribute is provided.

### What I Did

I can work around this by adding `nomonitor=False` to the invocation -- this isn't ideal since every souschef file will need to be updated.

I recommend we change `kwargs['nomonitor']` to `kwargs.get('nomonitor')` which will return None (assuming that's acceptable behavior) or explicitly set a default value with `kwargs.get('nomonitor', True)` or similarly for False.

@ivanistheone -- pinging since you'll probably have the best idea what the default should be.

I can work around the issue by removing empty lines from all YAML files that get merged by `charm build`: metadata.yaml, config.yaml, etc.

I can reproduce this issue by introducing empty lines in YAML files that get merged across multiple layers. For example, newlines after each option in a config.yaml.

I think it's possibly related to the different version of python-ruamel.yaml installed on xenial:
```
ii  python-ruamel.yaml                          0.10.23-1                                     amd64        roundtrip YAML parser/emitter (Python 2 module)
```

issue_title: processes started as root
issue_body:
On 3.4.10 (don't know when it started, it's working fine on 3.4.5) processes are started as root.
Depending on what you are doing hilarity ensues (e.g. writing json out to disk, the files will be owned by the wrong user etc.)
exabgp itself drops to nobody which then means that you can no longer stop exabgp because it tries to take down the forked processes which it no longer can do.

When killing a forked process exabgp will respawn it and of course now the process is running as nobody.

issue_title: Pytest only passes if device is named "PXI1Slot2"
task change event with nimi nidcpower sequence sweep
issue_body:
Hi,
I have the following use case. I define a sequence on a nidcpower device using the nimi nidcpower lib. during this sequence I need to observe if a digital input line changes its state. If the state changes I need to measure the nidcpower voltage.

Problem is, that the implementation of `wait_for_event(nidcpower.Event.SEQUENCE_ENGINE_DONE, 10)` is obviously blocking. Means that my callbacks for the registered events are lost. At least I do not enter the callback at all. So I refactored the implementation to use a thread for both, the nidaqmx task, and nidcpower `wait_for_event(nidcpower.Event.SEQUENCE_ENGINE_DONE, 10)` . Unfortunately the callback is still not invoked. Is this because the sync option in the c_func call for register_signal_event is always '0'? compare this help site
[http://zone.ni.com/reference/en-XX/help/370471AE-01/daqmxcfunc/daqmxregistersignalevent/](url)

If this is the issue, what would be the correct value for this option to be able to invoke the callback from within the thread?

Or is my use case not supported?

issue_title: Improve module test suite for filters.
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

#504 fixed an issue related to log entries not being formatted correctly.  That issue surfaced that we don't have very good testing against return values from filters for `pending` and `latest` filters.

### How can it be fixed?

Expand the test suite found at `web3/utils/module_testing/eth_module.py` to include a `pending` filter that includes one or more transaction hashes as well as a `latest` filter that includes one or more block hashes.

issue_title: Incorrect return value example for web3.personal.newAccount
issue_body:
http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web3.personal.html?highlight=newAccount#web3.personal.newAccount

The documentation provides the following example:

```
>>> web3.personal.newAccount('the-passphrase')
['0xd3cda913deb6f67967b99d67acdfa1712c293601']
```

However, the return value is the address as a string, not an array containing it:

```
>>> web3.personal.newAccount('the-passphrase')
'0xd3cda913deb6f67967b99d67acdfa1712c293601'
```

issue_title: Penman serialization
issue_body:
We now have a Penman module available (https://github.com/goodmami/penman) for serializing from arbitrary (but connected) triple lists, so it's a good time to add Penman serialization as an option for MRS.

It should be available for DMRS and EDS, particularly, but it could also be added for MRS (it's just an uglier graph). This could be done by giving the Mrs, Dmrs, and Eds classes `to_triples()` and `from_triples()` methods (like `to_dict()` and `from_dict()`). The penman module could then serialize them.

Thus, as the JSON serialization option requires two calls:

```python
json.dumps(Mrs.to_dict(x))
```

The Penman serialization would also require two:

```python
penman.dumps(Mrs.to_triples(x))
```

issue_title: Spurious error message when processing itsdb profiles
issue_body:
Using either `TestSuite.process()` or the `delphin process` command leads to an error message at the beginning of the process:

    ERROR:root:Malformed output from ACE: 

This seems to be caused by attempting to parse the trailing spaces (e.g., newline) as an s-expression. Besides the annoying message, it is harmless.

issue_title: Change default value for 'allow_softlinks' from True to False
issue_body: The `allow_softlinks` configuration parameter can be dangerous.  Softlinks into prefixes directly from the package cache will often lead to environment corruption.  It was an oversight that the default value for `allow_softlinks` is set to `True`.  In conda 4.4, we should definitely change the default value to `False`.  We should also consider it even for the next 4.3.x release.
