issue_title: New packages required for Debian 9
issue_body:
Debian 9 made the leap from OpenSSL 1.0 to 1.1.  Accordingly, the pre-compiled packages do not work with a plain-jane Debian 9 install:
```
urllib3.exceptions.SSLError: Can't connect to HTTPS URL because the SSL module is not available.
2017-07-06 15:50:34,008 DEBUG              elasticsearch       log_request_fail:105  > None
Unable to create client connection to Elasticsearch.  Error: ConnectionError(Can't connect to HTTPS URL because the SSL module is not available.) caused by: SSLError(Can't connect to HTTPS URL because the SSL module is not available.)
debian9-pkg-test $ openssl version
OpenSSL 1.1.0f  25 May 2017
```

Preliminary tests indicate that this will require a separate package, if not an entirely different repository.  I will have to figure out the best approach.  Compiling on Debian 9 works just fine, and the resultant package works as expected, _but only with Debian 9._  The two SSL libraries are definitely mutually exclusive.

I am unaware of any way, right now, to freeze in _multiple_ versions of the SSL library, and make it choose.  Were that possible, this would be easy.

She will get the following error

issue_title: Resource show fails on resources with parentheses in the name
issue_body:
### Description
Command `az resource show` with a resource name that includes parentheses returns "--resource-type was unexpected at this time" in PowerShell 5.0 console.

### Repro Steps

* Open PowerShell console
* Run `az resource show -g "TestGroup" --name "ResourceName(NestedForSomeReason)" --resource-type "Microsoft.OperationsManagement/solutions"` (resource type is example)

Result:

```
--resource-type was unexpected at this time.
C:\WINDOWS\system32>  "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\wbin\\..\python.exe" -Im azure.cli resource show
 -g TestGroup --name ResourceName(NestedForSomeReason) --resource-type Microsoft.OperationsManagement/solutions
```

### Remarks

Parentheses are currently allowed (as far as I know) in resource names, so the CLI should be able to handle them. I know that the parentheses should probably be escaped somehow, but the CLI does not allow for parentheses to be escaped. Neither PowerShell escaping with ``--name "ResourceName`(NestedForSomeReason`)"`` nor python escaping with `--name "ResourceName\(NestedForSomeReason\)"` works.

---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here: MSI

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here: 2.0.12

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: Windows 10 Enterprise

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: PowerShell 5.1.14

issue_title: SSH remote ipython notebook in detached mode using tmux
issue_body:
After starting ipython notebook on the server, opening it on the local machine works fine. However, when I try to combine tmux with it, the notebook fails to complete the work. The following is my workflow:
1. `SSH` to the server and start the ipython notebook with `tmux` session remotely.
2. Open the browser on the local machine and start a long-run task.
3. `detach` the session, exit `SSH` and good to go.

The above process is an ideal one, I got kernel shutdown in the end. The following is the message I got from the `tmux` session:

    [W 19:15:52.617 NotebookApp] Notebook simplified_algo.ipynb is not trusted      # The first time I left 
    [W 19:27:55.249 NotebookApp] WebSocket ping timeout after 119865 ms.  
    [I 22:09:13.696 NotebookApp] Saving file at /simplified_algo.ipynb                        # I then come back

    [W 22:15:18.390 NotebookApp] Notebook simplified_algo.ipynb is not trusted

issue_title: Add support for models that have different behavior at training/test time
issue_body:
From what I have seen, the KerasModel wrapper does not support models with different behaviors at training and test time, such as dropout or batch normalization. This is because there is no opportunity to pass in keras.backend.learning_phase() as a parameter to the prediction functions (defined in line 70, 72 of foolbox/models/keras.py). Trying to predict using a model that depends on the learning phase yields an error such as
InvalidArgumentError (see above for traceback): You must feed a value for placeholder tensor 'dropout_1/keras_learning_phase' with dtype bool
Code to reproduce error:
```
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dropout
import numpy as np
from foolbox.models import KerasModel

model = Sequential([Dropout(.1, input_shape = (1,))])
fmodel = KerasModel(model, (0,1))
fmodel.batch_predictions(np.ones((1,1)))

```

issue_title: grad is working, jacobian is not
issue_body:
First of all, autograd is an amazing tool! The grad function works great, but I am not able to make the `jacobian` function work.  I am on Python 3.4.3, numpy 1.10.1 and autograd 1.1.1 (pip).

The call to the `jacobian` function works, but the function it returns does not accept my argument and throws a `TypeError: The first input argument needs to be a sequence`

The error seems to occur in the concatenate function, defined at `line 44 in autograd/core.py` (although I do believe this error is actually an error raised by numpy). 

A minimum example to reproduce this:
```
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd import jacobian

x = np.array([5, 3], dtype=float)

def cost(x):
    return x[0]**2 / x[1] - np.log(x[1])

grad_cost = grad(cost)
jaco_cost = jacobian(cost)

print(grad_cost(x)) # works correctly!
print(jaco_cost(x)) # error
````
Another unrelated question (I am not sure if this is the correct place for this): If I wrote the above cost function in a separate module that imports numpy by itself (so not the autograd.numpy). Is it possible to somehow import this function from the module directly, instead of copy-pasting the code? Right now I get the following `AttributeError: 'FloatNode' object has no attribute 'log'`

The import is as follows:
```
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

#This module contains an 'import numpy as np' line
from my_module import cost

x = np.array([5, 3], dtype=float)
#Function from 'my_module' for which I'd like to calculate the gradient
grad_cost = grad(cost)
grad_cost(x) # error
```

If I remove the `np.log` call from my cost function the error disappears. So I guess I somehow need to override the 'regular' numpy import with the autograd numpy import, if possible.

issue_title: Array dimension expansion causes segfault
issue_body:
```
import numpy as np
from autograd import grad
def fun(x):
    return x[:, None].sum()
g = grad(fun)
g(np.random.randn(5))
```
Causes a seg fault. There are other ways to expand the dimensions of an array other than [:, None], but perhaps it should fail more gracefully in this case. I've also verified that it does the same thing with np.newaxis.

issue_title: Create wheels for CMake
issue_body:
Per discussion with CMake maintainers, a dedicated project named `CMakePythonPackages` (or `CMakePythonDistributions` or `CMakePythonWheels`) will be created.

It will contains the scripts allowing to create CMake binary (and source) wheels. 

For MacOSX and Windows, the binary wheels could be generated from installers available on https://cmake.org/download/

For Linux, CMake wheels could be built from source using manylinux docker image.

Within the wheel, CMake files could be packaged in either `cmake-<version>.data/platlib` or `cmake-<version>.data/data` (see [pep-0427](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/#installing-a-wheel-distribution-1-0-py32-none-any-whl))

issue_title: max_rdy_count hard-coded to 2500
issue_body:
In version 0.6.8:

AsyncConn.max_rdy_count is initialized to 2500 (line 175 in async.py) but the value from the actual nsqd server is never used. This means that Readers are effectively capped at 2500 max_in_flight.

It appears that the fix would be to parse max_rdy_count in AsyncConn._on_identify_response.

Reproduction steps:

1. `nsqd --max-rdy-count 10000 [...]`
2. Create a Python consumer: `nsq.Reader(..., max_in_flight=8000)`
3. Upon running the consumer, the following log message will appear: `WARNING:nsq.reader:[127.0.0.1:9999:POSTBACK] max RDY count 2500 < reader max in flight 8000, truncation possible`

issue_title: Question: PDB vs. SourceMap
issue_body:
https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/c9862ad13/Documentation/diagnostics/portable_pdb.md

says:

> .NET Core introduces a new symbol file (PDB) format - portable PDBs. Unlike traditional PDBs which are Windows-only, portable PDBs can be created and read on all platforms.

Was inventing the new PDB format for cross platform necessary when there is another well defined format that all browsers (and their debuggers), many editors and debuggers of various language platforms (beyond JavaScript and CSS) support these days:

https://sourcemaps.info/spec.html

Or am I missing the point and SourceMap and PDB are not comparable (or perhaps PDB offsets SourceMap functionality-wise)? If you have knowledge, can you please explain a bit what is not possible with SourceMap compared to [portable-]PDB?

1. Nginx plugin [requires `/etc/ssl/certs/ssl-cert-snakeoil.pem` and `/etc/ssl/private/ssl-cert-snakeoil.key`](https://github.com/letsencrypt/lets-encrypt-preview/blob/9394c26a9b532b6b8b5df1735114d60e5a1a9e27/letsencrypt_nginx/configurator.py#L273-278).
2. Nginx plugin fails whenever `nginx` fails to restart.
3. Nginx fails to restart when config file contains broken paths.
4. Snakeoil cert and key are created in post install hook of the `ssl-cert` Debian package. This package is not installed by `./bootstrap/debian.sh`.
5. Admin might simply delete snakeoil cert and key anyway.
6. We want to support systems other than Debian-derviatives.

Due to 5. and 6. it's not sufficient to update bootstrap scripts to `apt-get install ssl-cert`. I would suggest creating our own snakeoil certs in the process or deleting those `ssl_certificate` and `ssl_certificate_key` stanzas altogether. @diracdeltas?

Tested on fresh digitalocean 14.04 x64 droplet, reported by @jcjones.

issue_body:
On my tests, the settings.STATIC_URL it was not necessary to the Django find the complete URL, because it was found by the default STATICFILES_STORAGE inside the super class forms.Textarea. 
```
class CKEditorWidget(forms.Textarea):
    class Media:
        ...
        js += (
           settings.STATIC_URL + 'ckeditor/ckeditor/ckeditor.js',
            settings.STATIC_URL + 'ckeditor/ckeditor-init.js',
        )
        # The four lines above could be replaced by the lines below, because Django automatically finds the url through the STATICFILES_STORAGE
        js += (
           'ckeditor/ckeditor/ckeditor.js',
           'ckeditor/ckeditor-init.js',
        )
```

issue_title: Improve "required" validation; allow_none and allow_blank
issue_body:
Currently, setting `required=True` on Field only returns an error if the field's value is missing from the input data.

```python
class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str(required=True)
    
s = UserSchema()
s.validate({})  # {'name': ['Missing data for required field.']}
s.validate({'name': None})  #  No errors
s.validate({'name': ''})  # No errors
```

This may not meet the typical use case, where you want an error returned for `None` and the empty string (with the option to allow these values).

### Solution

By default, return an error if `None` or `''` are passed to a required field. Add `allow_none` and `allow_blank` options to allow these values to pass validation.

```python
UserSchema().validate({'name': None})  #  {'name': ['Missing data for required field.']}

class LenientSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str(required=True, allow_none=True)

LenientSchema().validate({'name': None})  # No errors
```

Note: This idea was yoinked from Django Rest Framework. =)

issue_title: How to systematically convert a marshmallow schema to an html form on the client?
issue_body:
I want to use marshmallow to represent, serialize, and deserialize various "types" of a schema. My specific use case is building forms for users to configure integration between their account on my application with their account on various other remote applications, each of which have a small handful of their own proprietary configuration details.

Ideally, I'd like to have a number of declarative schemas whose basic definitions could be passed via an API method to the client, which would intelligently render the form based on the schema definition (rather than writing each form in `HTML` by hand, and having to keep it in sync with marshmallow).

I was thinking of making a `marshmallow-jsonform` (see [ulion/jsonform](https://github.com/ulion/jsonform)) extension to accomplish this, but want to get a little feedback before re-inventing the wheel if there is some other better way to do what I am trying to do.

Does anyone have any suggestions?

issue_title: Add a timezone validator or field
issue_body:
Hi,

I found myself in the need to validate a timezone string (such as `Europe/Paris`) and didn't found a way to do it with Marshmallow. I think it's common enough for people to use these even though it seems this hasn't been discused before. Maybe we could add it ?

I volonteer to implement it if it pleases you, but I would have a few questions.

I would implement it using `pytz` since maintaining a comprehensive list of timezone must be out of question. But I see marshmallow doesn't have dependencies, is it something that you would agree to change ?

Also, would it make more sense to add a field or a validator ? A validator seems enough, but most time it would then be converted as a `tzinfo` instance, so maybe a field is better.

Thank you for your time!

issue_title: Unexpected behaviour in overwriting named config with command line when using ingredients
issue_body:
It is noted in the [docs](https://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/command_line.html#combination-with-regular-updates) that config given as command line arguments overwrite named config and config files. This works as expected for root configuration entries, but the overwrite unexpectedly does not happen for configuration entries of an ingredient. This occurs for both named config functions and configuration files.

Consider file `sacredtest.py`:
```
from sacred import Experiment, Ingredient

issue_body:
The following script exhibits the problem

    (declare-sort S 0)   
    (declare-fun g (Int S Int) Int)
    (declare-fun f (Int S Int Int) Bool)
    (assert
     (forall ((j Int)
             (i Int)
             (st S)
             (w Int)
             (wf Int))
      (let ((Ex (not (exists ((__a Int)) (= (g i st w) __a)))))
        (=> (and Ex (= wf 0))
            (f i st w j)))))
    (push)
The error is

issue_title: Make FlattenLayer support `None` in input shape
issue_body:
Currently, `FlattenLayer` does not work if any of the axes that are flattened have a shape of `None`. So this does not work:

```
l = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((None, None, None))
l = lasagne.layers.FlattenLayer(l)
```

Instead, users get an unhelpful error message because Lasagne attempts to compute the output shape nevertheless: 

```
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'int' and 'NoneType'
```

(see https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/lasagne-users/0fa-GRH8wwo )

The shape computation is here: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/shape.py#L46-L49

I think we can easily adapt this to simply return `None` for the flattened dimension of any of the constituent dimensions from the input shape have size `None`.

Any takers? :) If nobody wants to I can probably do a PR this weekend. Shouldn't be too much work, but I don't have much time to spare at the moment.

issue_title: Where is the the Python (3) kernel?
issue_body:
interact is working for me with the Python kernel but I have a question.

Seeking clarification on the location of things.

I think I have `kernel.json` in two locations:
1. `/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json`
2. `/Users/lukejanicke/Library/Jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json`

Here is my installation procedure.

1. I installed Python 3 with Homebrew.

```
brew install python3
```

2. [**nteract** website recommends](https://nteract.io/kernels/python) installing the Python kernel with the following commands.

```
python -m pip install ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user
```

The first command did not work for me.

```
~ $ python -m pip install ipykernel
/usr/bin/python: No module named pip
```

A few other attempts also failed.

```
~ $ python3 -m pip3 install ipykernel
/usr/local/opt/python3/bin/python3.6: No module named pip3
```

Until I found something that worked.

```
~ $ pip3 install ipykernel
Collecting ipykernel
. . .
Successfully installed appnope-0.1.0 decorator-4.1.2 ipykernel-4.7.0 ipython-6.2.1 ipython-genutils-0.2.0 jedi-0.11.1 jupyter-client-5.2.1 jupyter-core-4.4.0 parso-0.1.1 pexpect-4.3.1 pickleshare-0.7.4 prompt-toolkit-1.0.15 ptyprocess-0.5.2 pygments-2.2.0 python-dateutil-2.6.1 pyzmq-16.0.3 simplegeneric-0.8.1 six-1.11.0 tornado-4.5.3 traitlets-4.3.2 wcwidth-0.1.7
```

I later found that `python3 -m pip install ipykernel` also works.

At this point, `jupyter kernelspec list` gave the following output.

```
~ $ jupyter kernelspec list
Available kernels:
  python3    /usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/python3
```

I ran the next comment from the [**nteract** website](https://nteract.io/kernels/python), changing `python` to `python3`.

```
~$ python3 -m ipykernel install --user
Installed kernelspec python3 in /Users/lukejanicke/Library/Jupyter/kernels/python3
```

At this point, I tried to run **nteract** and the Python kernel was not starting.

I ran `jupyter kernelspec list` again and noticed a different result!

```
$ jupyter kernelspec list
Available kernels:
  python3    /Users/lukejanicke/Library/Jupyter/kernels/python3
```

This confused me, because I remember from the last time I did this that I had to go into `kernel.json` and change the first `argv` from `python` to `python3`.

But now I had two `kernel.json` files.

1. `/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json`
2. `/Users/lukejanicke/Library/Jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json`

`kernel.json` (2) already said `python3` and **nteract** was still not starting the Python kernel. So naturally, I had to edit `kernel.json` (1)

I edited `kernel.json` (1) to say `python3` as below.

```
{
 "argv": [
  "python3",
  "-m",
  "ipykernel_launcher",
  "-f",
  "{connection_file}"
 ],
 "display_name": "Python 3",
 "language": "python"
}
```

After this, the **nteract** started with the Python kernel and seems to be working correctly.

Back to my question.

Why did `jupyter kernelspec list` give a different result between the following two commands?

```
pip3 install ipykernel
python3 -m ipykernel install --user
```

And why are there two `kernel.json` files?

issue_title: Loading of binary with lots of symbols is slow
issue_body:
I've tried to load a PE binary with 27000 symbol, and it took 90seconds. 
Did some profiling:
![perf](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/759365/19621382/d8771ff2-9890-11e6-998f-454cf72cce16.png)

It turned out that the code spends a lot of time in the `provide_symbol` function in `cle/loader.py`. Its because the lookup for the symbols is done by a a loop (O(n)), and since we look up n symbols we end up with (O(n^)) runtime.

```
for reloc in obj.relocs:
  if reloc.symbol and reloc.symbol.name == name:
    reloc.relocate(solist)
```

I've did a quick patch to build a map from the symbols, and with this the time to call `provide_symbol` reduces greatly:
![perf2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/759365/19621440/51d1ae2a-9892-11e6-8898-5e151a6ed58c.png)

I didn't sent a pull-request because the patch is just a proof-of-concept and not done correctly.

Could you fix this?

issue_title: Don't hardcode vex register offsets
issue_body:
We've been advised by the valgrind people that those offsets are not meant to be stable across platforms, or even across compilers. The correct solution is to have a method in pyvex that extracts the current platform's offsets from libvex (this can be via a function in pyvex_native that simply does a bunch of `offsetof` calculations) and if pyvex is available at import time, use that to populate the register fields.

This will drastically simplify the maintenance of archinfo.

issue_title: Default grid directory names
issue_body:
Hi,

When Ganga submits to the GRID (in my case Dirac) it is very difficult to connect LFNs with Ganga jobs. This makes managing GRID space extremely difficult and time consuming for the user, and many don't bother, leading to enormous wastage of disk space. 

Please can we make a trivial change to include the Ganga job id (not even the subjob, necessarily) in the LFN path? It would be even better to have the name of the ganga job there too, but perhaps that is asking too much. 

It would be vastly preferable if this could happen by default.

Thanks!
Dan

issue_title: Schemas validation
issue_body:
Let's start a discussion about schema validation !


I believe we want something similar to *Daybed*. Except that we won't
reinvent the wheel with custom schema formalism.

It looks like there is no way around JSON schema. We won't rewrite thousands
of lines of code using Colander (see https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema).
We will use existing validators, and frontends will build forms from schemas.

By default, collections could be schemaless. 

```html

PUT|PATCH|DELETE /collections/person/schema
{
    {
        "title": "Example Schema",
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "firstName": {
                "type": "string"
            },
            "lastName": {
                "type": "string"
            },
            "age": {
                "description": "Age in years",
                "type": "integer",
                "minimum": 0
            }
        },
        "required": ["firstName", "lastName"]
    }
}

```

It is acceptable if the validation of the definition is done using Cornice/Colander.
Even though there are [meta-schemas](http://json-schema.org/documentation.html) :)

If a schema exists for a collection, records that don't match the schema are
refused with ``400 Bad Request`` (on PUT and after merge of PATCH).

Unlike Daybed, I propose that each user owns the schema of the collection.
Especially because the schema endpoint will probably be a resource :)
Schemas could be cached to avoid overhead of reading it from storage at each incoming record.

This implies that users can store heterogeneous data with the same collection name.

Hence, on first start, the JS application will have to check that the user did
not set any schema (e.g. at ``/collections/moz:readinglist:articles/schema``).
If she did, then confirm to replace it. If she hacks it afterwards and stores
invalid records, the JS application may crash, but that's okay.

Open questions

* Is the schema using the resource code of Cliquet ? If so, how do avoid overlap of stored collections ? We could use underscores prefix, and prevent public collections to have a name that starts with underscore :)
* What happen to existing data when schema is changed ? Probably ignore and wait for next
  PUT or PATCH ?
* What happen when records are shared between users ? Do we let other users
  records crash our application when fetching shared records ? We could probably
  run client-side validation using JSON schema on shared records.
* Do we want to provide a collection of custom formats or even types ?
  I'm thinking of recurrent needs for uuid4, geohash, GeoJSON objects, phone, postal codes...

## Versions

Credstash is installed in a docker container based on openjdk:7-jdk-alpine using the following steps:

```
apk --no-cache add python3 python3-dev libffi-dev openssl-dev build-base 
pip3 install credstash

- boto3-1.7.56 
- botocore-1.10.56 
- cffi-1.11.5 
- credstash-1.14.0

- pycparser-2.18

### Broken combination from July 13th onwards

issue_title: Windows 10 Version 1607 causes camera to crash
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version |  1.15.00 /  1.17.00   | 
| Operating System & Version |  Windows 10 : version 1607	os build 14393.51  |
| Build System |  VS2015 | 

The librealses seems to have problems with the recent windows 10 Build (version 1607) release on the 2.8.2016 .

I experince some random crashes of the camera. I could not pin done the exact line causing the issue but would recommand not to update to the current build yet.

(In case someone experinces the same problem, there is an option to revert the system back to the latest build ( without reinstalling the system). Officialy not available after 10 days with the new build.

UPDATE:
I testet this issue on different maschines :
 1. Running old build of win 10 -> no problems with firmeware 1.15 /1.17
 2. Running current build of win 10 -> error while init camera and set parameters
    -> After reset to old build of win 10 -> no problems with firmeware 1.15 /1.17 anymore.
3. Running current old build of win 10 ->  no problems with firmeware 1.15 /1.17
   -> After installing current build ->  error while init camera and set parameters

So this problems seems to be connected to the current windows build.

The error seems to be thrown here [uvc.wmf line 564] (https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/src/uvc-wmf.cpp#L564)
when setting a F200/SR300 parameter.

`ks_control->KsProperty((PKSPROPERTY)&node, sizeof(KSP_NODE), data, len, nullptr)`
allways returns -2147467261 (HRESULT : E_POINTER Invalid pointer. ) which indicates an error.
Same program with previos build returns 0 (indicates no error).

If i do ignore those error some parameters are still beeing set to the camera. However it is not possible to check if it was executed correctly ( which usually results in a retry till it worked)

Is it possible, that some interfaces were changed at this point (windows side) ? 

SOLVED:

Problem was that 
`ks_control->KsProperty((PKSPROPERTY)&node, sizeof(KSP_NODE), data, len, nullptr)` does not accept nullpointer anymore. Changed to 

```
ULONG bytes_received = 0;
			check("IKsControl::KsProperty", ks_control->KsProperty((PKSPROPERTY)&node, sizeof(KSP_NODE), data, len, &bytes_received));
```

issue_title: Support Storm's custom serialization support for multi-lang. [msgpack]
issue_body:
_From @amontalenti on May 6, 2014 16:30_

A recently [merged pull request 84 on the Storm project][merged] implements custom serialization support for the multi-lang protocol. This means that you can technically register other faster serializers than JSON for Storm's tuple data, e.g. we could use [msgpack][msgpack].

In addition, thanks to Storm's built-in support for serialization hooks with Kyro, we could actually switch Tuple serialization from Kyro's built-in JVM-based serializer to something like msgpack. This would potentially allow us to avoid the encode/decode cost associated with using ShellBolts and ShellSpouts.

It would be great for someone from the community to do a prototype of this using a streamparse project, and prove its viability. Then we could merge the support into the core library, build a support library in Java for the custom serializers, and offer documentation for how to set up Storm to leverage them. This will likely fit in very well with our lein-based Storm project management.

[merged]: https://github.com/apache/incubator-storm/pull/84
[msgpack]: http://msgpack.org/

_Copied from original issue: Parsely/streamparse#8_

issue_title: Add certbot-route53 to our tree
issue_body:
After talking to the maintainers we're going to make https://github.com/lifeonmarspt/certbot-route53 an official plugin and move it into our repo. Things we want with this transition are:

* Git history is preserved
* We have a plugin under the name certbot-dns-route53 in our tree
* We have a plugin or at least something to upload to the name certbot-route53 on PyPI so users under that name continue to get updates as the plugin is further developed. In theory, this could be the same package as `certbot-dns-route53` but we'd want to make sure the user experience is sane if both are installed.
* The certbot-route53 package should continue to work with the plugin name `certbot-route53:auth` due to the way we name 3rd party plugins (see #4351). Whether or not it also works with `certbot-route53` is an implementation detail in my opinion.

I don't really have a preference for how this is implemented. My instinct is it'll be easiest to make `certbot-route53` a shim package around `certbot-dns-route53`. We did something similar for the `letsencrypt` package in our 0.7.0 release. This can be left up to whoever takes this ticket though.

issue_title: Improve error message for external tasks
issue_body:
I find the error prompt here is not ideal:

```
ERROR: 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 failed with RuntimeError error Failed to execute script (ret=1).
Please use command
	Rscript \
	  --default-packages=datasets,methods,utils,stats,grDevices,graphics \
	  /home/gaow/tmp/test/.sos/core_fl_0_16_e3e26db7.R
under "/home/gaow/tmp/test" to test it.
```

For this example the computation is quite heavy. It takes up to an hour to reproduce the error. When executed as non-task the error message would dump on the screen, which is ugly but can give people some hints. 

However here apparently one can see the error message via `sos status 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 -v 4`. That opens the err file which has more information including the prompt above. That can save people from re-running. I'm wondering if it is possible to distinguish between task/non-task case and ask users to use `sos status` to check the job, instead of suggesting a rerun.

issue_title: find() / findall() do client-side filtering and could be optimized
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587821, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20feature', 'name': 'type: feature', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The implementations of `find()` and `findall()` in `BaseResourceManager` retrieve all objects and then apply the specified filtering. However, the HMC supports server-side filtering by means of specifying query parameters on the URL.

In order to optimize performance, I suggest to change this to exploit the server-side filtering.

One option to implement this could be to add the filtering capability to `list()` and then to exploit it in `find()` and `findall()`.

issue_title: Error during WebSocket handshake: Sent non-empty 'Sec-WebSocket-Protocol' header but no response was received
issue_body:
The WebSockify bridge of noVNC seems to be broken since commit 4099949984eb80ef33c2d0dd216991124975a5d2.

The error that appears in Chrome's console is:

```js
websock.js:278 WebSocket connection to 'wss://janitor.technology/websockify' failed: Error during WebSocket handshake: Sent non-empty 'Sec-WebSocket-Protocol' header but no response was received
```

I had a look at the headers received from WebSockify's server, and indeed it seems that `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` is no longer being sent.

Before 4099949984eb80ef33c2d0dd216991124975a5d2:

```
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Server: WebSockify Python/2.7.12
Date: Tue, 07 Feb 2017 15:52:33 GMT
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: yHBB/hXTWGaTjk29VQhWk2not24=
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: binary
```

After 4099949984eb80ef33c2d0dd216991124975a5d2:

```
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: ZefPJQjbgtmJGvzKFp7JvLWpDvI=
```

Maybe it could help to continue sending `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: binary` in the WebSocket headers? Or am I doing something wrong here?

issue_title: Dropdown ipywidget after unobserve_all() does not accept any further observe()
issue_body:
I use ipywidget 7.0.5 (py35_0)
The dropdown ipywidget after unobserve_all() does not work for any further observe().
This is easily reproducible with the following code:

```
import ipywidgets.widgets as widgets
from IPython.display import display
def helloWorld(_=None):
    print("Hello World!")

# the dropdown is not working properly
widget = widgets.Dropdown( 
            options=[('One', 1), 
                     ('Two', 2),
                     ('Three', 3)],
            description='dropdown_w:',
            value = 2
        )
widget.observe(helloWorld, 'value')
display(widget)
widget.unobserve_all()
widget.observe(helloWorld, 'value')


## instead same code works for other widgets (as it should)
w = widget = widgets.FloatSlider(value = 0.5, min = 0, max = 1, step = 0.1, description = "w")
w.observe(helloWorld, 'value')
display(w)
w.unobserve_all()
w.observe(helloWorld, 'value')
```

issue_title: Tests rely on `at exit` behaviour which does not happen until far in the future.
issue_body:
Running `pytest --basic`, I see a lot of heavy stuff created by tests but then left around to be cleaned up by "at exit" cleanup, which doesn't happen until the whole test suite has run.

For example, with --log-cli=DEBUG,

    swift_t.py                 105 DEBUG    [RUNNER] Queue is empty

is logged every few seconds even after all the tests have completed and pytest shutdown is happening - presumably from an earlier swift_t test.

I also see a lot of this:

    2018-05-23 20:34:44 parsl.dataflow.strategy:155 [DEBUG]  Site:local_threads Status:STATIC

... which might be from a lot of DFKs sitting round doing nothing.

Also, a bunch of `ipcontrollers` are left and (supposedly) cleaned up by at exit behaviour (see another issue/PR I have yet to write).

It would probably be better if all of these things were cleaned up more explicitly: with explicit DFK cleanup in each test, and with that DFK cleanup really cleaning up everything it should.

issue_body:
Hello, 

If I remember correctly similar issue was already raised some time ago but was set to be resolved. However I got the warnings below (both on linux (Debian 9, both python 2.7 and 3.5) and on Windows 10 (recent Anaconda). 
```
Running Sphinx v1.7.1
WARNING: while setting up extension sphinx.ext.autodoc: directive 'automodule' is already registered, it will be overridden

WARNING: while setting up extension sphinx.ext.autodoc: directive 'autoinstanceattribute' is already registered, it will be overridden
```
Changing the order of sphinx extensions (putting autodoc before sphinxcontrib.matlab)  results in the crash:
```
Exception occurred:
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/logging/__init__.py", line 1179, in warning
    self._log(WARNING, msg, args, **kwargs)
TypeError: _log() got an unexpected keyword argument 'line'

WARNING: invalid signature for automodule (u'+mp')

This seems logical as it looks some stuff from sphinxcontrib.matlab is going to be overridden. (I have matlab_src_dir set correctly). Before digging what is wrong, maybe someone can confirm the problems?
Thank you in advance for support.
Regards
Roman
